title: Creating Stories, Worlds, and a Giant Box of Battle Mats! (Solo RPG)
body:
https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=ath0o3oOJCs

[Summary]

Daniel (@dungeondive) unboxes three handsome tools for world-building and storytelling: the Story Engine Deck, the Deck of Worlds (both from storyenginedeck.com), and the Map Library from Roll and Play. The Story Engine Deck uses five card categories—agents (characters), engines (motivations), anchors (objects/locations), conflicts, and aspects (adjectives)—to generate prompts that cascade into characters, encounters, and plot hooks. Daniel demonstrates by drawing a hermit-turned-holy-person who discovers a saint's grave is empty, disrupting everything their monastery believes. The Deck of Worlds excels at generating micro-locations: drawing region, landmark, namesake, origin, attribute, and advent cards to birth detailed settings rich with lore and potential. Daniel's moraine temple of river monks, shaped by constantly shifting glacial terrain and founded by a famous book, practically begs to be explored. The Map Library—a gorgeous $80 box holding 100 vinyl battle mats across arctic, coastal, desert, forest, underground, and urban settings—provides the physical stage once your worlds are imagined. Each mat is double-sided and lavishly detailed, perfect for five leagues or forbidden psalms-style skirmishes. Daniel loved all three, already ordering expansions because these tools genuinely spark imagination.

----

Which sounds more valuable to you for your next campaign—a card-based world generator to birth entire settings, or a library of ready-to-play battle maps?
